title: Git Workflow
text:
Warning



Ensure you have completed the steps in Installation before attempting to do the tutorial on this page!

title: Git Workflow
text:
Introduction to Version Control
Now that you’ve finished installing all the necessary software, it’s time to integrate Git into your workflow! As stated before, Git is a version control software designed to track changes in files. This feature will be extremely helpful as your research project evolves over time.

You can imagine that there may be certain points in your project where you are investigating several different directions and it becomes hard to keep track of what has already happened. You may even want to undo or redo some steps as you get feedback, like the comic above illustrates.
With Git, all your project history is cleanly recorded, allowing you easy access to any point in your history without cluttering your computer with all of the file iterations that precede the final version. Additionally, Git allows collaborators to jump in and safely contribute to your project in an organized manner, making it a highly versatile and valuable software to use.

title: Git Workflow
text:
Git vs. GitHub
Git and GitHub have similar names, but it’s important to note the differences in their function!
Git:


is an open-source distributed version control software, designed to manage the versioning of files
operates locally on your computer, and can be enabled on a specific folder/directory to version the files inside

this tracked folder is called a repository or “repo” for short

includes features such as creating repositories, committing changes, branching and merging code
primarily used via command line code, but there are also many GUIs like the RStudio Git feature

GitHub:


is an online platform/website built around Git
provides a centralized location to host and share Git repos in the cloud
offers additional features on top of Git, such as a web-based interface, issue tracking, project management tools, pull requests, and code review

title: Git Workflow
text:
Workflow Overview
The typical Git workflow can be daunting for a beginner to figure out how all these moving parts work together, so here is a brief overview, simplified as much as possible.
Suppose we have a project folder on our local computer, my_project that contains our scripts, data_download.R, data_prep.R and data_analysis.R like so:
my_project/            # Our working directory
├── data_download.R    # First working file 
│    
├── data_prep.R        # Second working file 
│
└── data_analysis.R    # Third working file        
In this example, my_project is our working directory and the R scripts are our local working files, which are the files that we edit as we work.
Now suppose we want to start using Git to track the changes in the my_project folder. To do this, the command is called initializing my_project as a Git repository. By initializing a folder as a Git repo, we are telling Git to start tracking all the contents inside this folder.
This initialization step will create a hidden .git folder, which is the actual Git repository that will have the history of our files. This Git repo is also referred to as our local repo since it is on our local computer.
my_project/            # Our working directory
├── data_download.R    # First working file 
│    
├── data_prep.R        # Second working file 
│
├── data_analysis.R    # Third working file
│
└── .git/              # Our local repo that will have 
    └── ...            # the saved history of our files






Note



By the way, the way to tell the difference between a normal folder and a folder tracked by Git is precisely the presence of that hidden .git folder! If you’re unsure whether a folder has been initialized by Git already, you can change your computer’s settings to enable seeing hidden folders and check if the folder has a .git folder inside.


Then as we edit our working files, we can use Git commands to save versions of our files locally. Once we’re satisfied, we can finally send our edits to GitHub, a popular destination to host remote repos (repositories that live in the cloud).
The diagram below illustrates the Git commands needed to carry out the basic workflow.

In the diagram, rabbits are moving back and forth between 2 islands: an island with the remote repo (representing the cloud) and an island with the working directory + local repo (representing the local computer).
Starting in the bottom right-hand corner, the rabbits in the working directory move file boxes to the staging area with the add command. Then they move the file boxes from the staging area to the local repo with the commit command.
Finally, the rabbits get in a boat to move the boxes from the local repo to the remote repo with the push command. To move boxes from the remote repo back to the local repo, they use the pull command. There is also a dotted line in the center to signify that it’s best practice to pull before pushing.
Although it’s common to initialize a Git repo on your local computer first and then send it to GitHub, you can also go the opposite way! Some people may find it easier to start the remote repo on GitHub first and then clone it to their local computer. Either method will work and they’re both valid. This tutorial will focus on the latter method.

title: Git Workflow
text:
Git Vocabulary Summary
To summarize, here are all the basic Git vocabulary terms covered thus far and their respective commands:








Term
Git Command
Definition




Initialize
git init
initializes a brand new local Git repo (*)


Add/Stage
git add [file]
marks the local modified file(s) going into the next commit by putting them in the staging area


Commit
git commit -m "[my commit message]"
captures a snapshot of currently staged local changes with a message describing what exactly has been changed; this snapshot goes into our local repo


Pull
git pull
grabs changes from a remote repo and merges them into your local working file(s)


Push
git push
sends local changes to a remote repo


Clone
git clone [repo]
creates a local copy of a remote repo (*)



(*) You can start your workflow either by initializing an already existing local folder as a Git repo and then pushing to GitHub, OR creating a remote repo on GitHub and then cloning it to your local computer!
If you would like to see the full list of commands, visit the Git documentation.
Don’t worry about memorizing the exact commands, we can utilize the GUIs available in RStudio, VS Code, or GitHub Desktop to carry out the same operations with simple button clicks.

title: Git Workflow
text:
Practice
Now let’s practice the Git workflow by first creating a remote repo on GitHub and then cloning it to our local computer!

Creating a repo on GitHub
Log into GitHub. Once you’re logged in, click on your account icon in the top right corner and go to “Repositories”.

Click on “New” to create your new repository.

Fill out your practice repo name, write a short description to summarize what this repo contains, set the visibility to “Public”, add a README, and add a gitignore template that corresponds to the programming language you will use for this exercise. There are tons of gitignore template options for various languages, like R, Python, Java, C++, etc.






Note



A gitignore is a file that does exactly what it says— it tells Git to ignore certain files! While working on your project, there may be certain files or file types you don’t wish to track the changes of, in which case, you can list them inside the gitignore. For example, if your code exports a lot of png files that don’t need to be tracked by Git, you can type “*.png” in the gitignore to instruct Git to ignore all files with the “.png” extension.



We won’t add a license to this practice repo but for actual research projects in MDEL, the most common license type is the MIT License. Check out the GitHub documentation on licenses to learn more.
Once the form is filled out, click “Create repository” and wait for the process to finish. Your new practice repo may look like this:

The landing page shows the top folders/files in your repo as well as some information on when those files were modified. Right now, the repo only has a gitignore and README file that were created just a moment ago.
If you’re curious about the function of a gitignore, click on the file and see what file types are inside for your specified coding language. For example, here are the file types for the R gitignore template:

Miscellaneous files like .Rhistory and .Rdata are listed inside. If you’re a regular R user, you may recognize these file types as the files that get frequently get created by R during your session. These files don’t impact your actual work so it makes sense for Git to ignore them.


Cloning to your local computer
To get a copy of this remote repo to our local computer, we will need to clone it. The Git command to do so is git clone, but IDEs like RStudio and VS Code provide easy-to-use interfaces that allow you to do the same operation with button clicks.
If you don’t use RStudio or VS Code, feel free to try out GitHub Desktop instead.

RStudioVS CodeGitHub Desktop


Go to the landing page of your practice repo on GitHub. Click on the “Code” then copy the link under HTTPS in the dropdown menu.

Switch to RStudio. Click on the top right corner that has an icon of a box with an R on it.

In the resulting dropdown menu, click “New Project”.

Then select “Version Control”.

And then “Git”.

In the following page, paste the link you copied from GitHub. For the local copy of your repo, you can name it a different name from your remote repo if you wish, but it’s generally recommended to keep the name the same. You can also choose where to clone the repo on your computer. If you wish to easily remember what folders are Git repos versus normal folders on your computer, you can create a folder called “GitHub” where all your local Git repos can live.

Otherwise, once you’re satisfied, click “Create Project”.
Now we have cloned the repo to our local computer! Notice in the top right corner that we’re now working inside the practice repo, and that we have local copies of the gitignore and README.



Go to the landing page of your practice repo on GitHub. Click on the “Code” then copy the link under HTTPS in the dropdown menu.

Switch to VS Code. Either click on the Source Control tab on the left-hand side and click “Clone Repository” or click “Clone Git Repository…” from the landing page.

The bar at the top will pop-up.

Paste the link you copied from GitHub and then click “Clone from URL …”

Your file explorer should appear, letting you choose where to clone your repo. If you wish to easily remember what folders are Git repos versus normal folders on your computer, you can create a folder called “GitHub” where all your local Git repos can live.

Otherwise, once you’re satisfied, click “Select as Repository Destination”.
Now we have cloned the repo to our local computer! You will get another pop-up asking if you would like to open the repo. Click “Open”.

Notice at the top that we’re now working inside the practice repo, and that we have local copies of the gitignore and README.



Open GitHub Desktop. Make sure you’re logged in if you haven’t done so already. The landing page should show a list of your repos.

Select your practice repo from the list and click “[Clone your_username]/[your_practice_repo]”.

A new menu should appear letting you choose where to clone your repo with the “Choose…” button. If you wish to easily remember what folders are Git repos versus normal folders on your computer, you can create a folder called “GitHub” where all your local Git repos can live.
Otherwise, once you’re satisfied, click “Clone”.

Now we have cloned the repo to our local computer!

If you click on “History”, you can inspect the first commit in this repo, where we added our gitignore and README files.

You can also manually check that your computer has a complete clone of this repo by going to your file explorer.






Pushing a local edit to GitHub
Let’s practice a simple Git workflow where we create a new file on our local computer and send it to GitHub.

RStudioVS CodeGitHub Desktop


Start by creating a new practice script in RStudio. Feel free to write any code in the script as this is just an example.

Once you’re satisfied, click on the “Git” tab and then click on either the “Diff” or “Commit” tabs. These two tabs will lead to the same place, so don’t worry about clicking on either one.

A new window should pop up. Click on your practice script and notice how there are green lines highlighted in the window. The green lines signify the new additions to this file since our last commit. Since this file has never been tracked by Git before, all the lines are highlighted in green and there is a yellow question mark icon next to the script name.

To explain more about what these icons mean, they represent the status of each file in your repo. The yellow question mark signifies a file that is not yet tracked by Git. Here are some other icons you might see working in RStudio:

If you think back to the Git Workflow Overview, in order to send this practice script to GitHub, the first step is to add it to the staging area. The Git command for this is git add, but thankfully, we can do the equivalent operation by checking the checkbox to the left of the script name.
Notice how the yellow question mark icon turned into a green “A” icon, which represents a new file that is now marked for tracking.
You can imagine that in a regular project, you may want to stage several files in a single commit by checking multiple checkboxes, but we will only focus on staging this one file for this exercise.
After staging the file, we will need to commit it, which will capture a snapshot of any currently staged local changes. The corresponding Git command for this is git commit -m "[my commit message]", but we can use the RStudio GUI instead. Write a commit message to describe exactly what we’ve changed in the right-hand panel, and then click “Commit”.

Once you click “Commit”, you might see a new window pop-up like this:

Close the pop-up window to get back to your main workspace and you will see a message that says, “Your branch is ahead of ‘origin/main’ by 1 commit.” This means that we have a local commit that the remote repo on GitHub does not have yet. In a regular project, you would probably have several commits before you’re ready to push, but for the purposes of this exercise, we will send this one commit to GitHub.

To send this local commit to GitHub, we will want to pull (git pull) first and then push (git push). Pulling will fetch the latest changes on GitHub and merge them with our local files. It’s best practice to pull often and to always pull BEFORE pushing. It makes sense to grab the latest version of the files on GitHub and then make our local edits on top of that. After we make our local edits, then we can push them back to GitHub. This will create a clean history of your repo.
This habit will really save you whenever you’re working in a repo with other collaborators, who may make edits when you’re away from your computer. By pulling frequently, you can avoid merge conflicts. You can always pull at any point in your workflow, so don’t hesitate to make it a habit.
With that said, click on the “Pull” down arrow to fetch and merge the latest changes from GitHub.

It will say that we’re already up to date because we haven’t done anything to our GitHub repo since we’ve last touched it.

Close that window and click “Push” up arrow to send our local changes to GitHub.

If you see a pop-up window that looks kind of like this, you’ve successfully pushed to GitHub!

You can confirm by navigating back to your GitHub repo. Check to see that you now have a second commit where you created your practice script. If you click on where it says “2 Commits”, you can view the history of all your commits.

Click on your latest commit.
 You should be able to see the green lines again showing the new additions to this file.



Start by creating a new practice script in VS Code. Feel free to write any code in the script as this is just an example.

You might notice after saving your script that the Source Control tab has a “1” alert on it. Click on it.

You will be led to these panels where we can do our Git workflow and keep track of the status of our files. Notice how our script is listed under “Changes”, with a “U” icon next to the script name.

To explain more about what these icons mean, they represent the status of each file in your repo. The “U” icon signifies a file that is not yet tracked by Git. Here are some other icons you might see working in VS Code:

If you think back to the Git Workflow Overview, in order to send this practice script to GitHub, the first step is to add it to the staging area. The Git command for this is git add, but thankfully, we can do the equivalent operation by clicking the “+” to the right of the script name.

Notice how the “U” icon turned into an “A” icon, which represents a new file that is now marked for tracking.
You can imagine that in a regular project, you may want to stage several files in a single commit by clicking multiple “+”s, but we will only focus on staging this one file for this exercise.

After staging the file, we will need to commit it, which will capture a snapshot of any currently staged local changes. The corresponding Git command for this is git commit -m "[my commit message]", but we can use the VS Code GUI instead. Write a commit message to describe exactly what we’ve changed at the top, and then click “Commit”.

You’ll now see that the panel is showing a “Sync Changes” button. This button will perform a pull and a push into a single operation. You may want to hold off on using this button until you’re more familiar with the Git workflow. Instead, let’s do a pull and a push separately.
Also, notice that we now have a local commit that the remote repo on GitHub does not have yet. In a regular project, you would probably have several commits before you’re ready to push, but for the purposes of this exercise, we will send this one commit to GitHub.

To send this local commit to GitHub, we will want to pull (git pull) first and then push (git push). Pulling will fetch the latest changes on GitHub and merge them with our local files. It’s best practice to pull often and to always pull BEFORE pushing. It makes sense to grab the latest version of the files on GitHub and then make our local edits on top of that. After we make our local edits, then we can push them back to GitHub. This will create a clean history of your repo.
This habit will really save you whenever you’re working in a repo with other collaborators, who may make edits when you’re away from your computer. By pulling frequently, you can avoid merge conflicts. You can always pull at any point in your workflow, so don’t hesitate to make it a habit.
With that said, click on the Pull down arrow to fetch and merge the latest changes from GitHub.

Nothing will happen; we’re already up to date because we haven’t done anything to our GitHub repo since we’ve last touched it.
Click Push up arrow to send our local changes to GitHub.

A pop-up window will appear asking if you want to sign in using GitHub. This is the very last authentication step required to connect VS Code and GitHub. Click “Allow”.

Your browser will redirect you to a new tab, asking you to authorize VS Code. Click “Continue”.

Then click “Authorize Visual-Studio-Code”.

Click “Open Visual Studio Code” to get redirected back to VS Code.

You’ve successfully pushed to GitHub!

You can confirm by navigating back to your GitHub repo. Check to see that you now have a second commit where you created your practice script. If you click on where it says “2 Commits”, you can view the history of all your commits.

Click on your latest commit.

You should be able to see the green lines showing the new additions to this file.



Start by creating a new practice script in your preferred IDE. Feel free to write any code in the script as this is just an example. For instance, Spyder is a popular IDE for Python and can be used to write new Python scripts.

Once you’ve saved your edits in your preferred IDE, your GitHub Desktop screen should now look something like below. Notice how there are green lines highlighted in the window. The green lines signify the new additions to this file since our last commit. Since this file has never been tracked by Git before, all the lines are highlighted in green and there is a green “+” icon next to the script name.

To explain more about what these icons mean, they represent the status of each file in your repo. The green “+” icon signifies a file that is not yet tracked by Git. Here are some other icons you might see in GitHub Desktop:

If you think back to the Git Workflow Overview, in order to send this practice script to GitHub, the first step is to add it to the staging area. The Git command for this is git add, but thankfully, we can do the equivalent operation by checking the checkbox to the left of the script name.
You can imagine that in a regular project, you may want to stage several files in a single commit by checking multiple checkboxes, but we will only focus on staging this one file for this exercise.
After staging the file, we will need to commit it, which will capture a snapshot of any currently staged local changes. The corresponding Git command for this is git commit -m "[my commit message]", but we can use the GitHub Desktop GUI instead. Write a commit message to describe exactly what we’ve changed in the left-hand panel, and then click “Commit 1 file to main”.

Once you click “Commit 1 file to main”, you will see an alert that says, “Committed just now”, followed by your commit message. This means that we have a local commit that the remote repo on GitHub does not have yet. In a regular project, you would probably have several commits before you’re ready to push, but for the purposes of this exercise, we will send this one commit to GitHub.

To send this local commit to GitHub, we will want to pull (git pull) first and then push (git push). Pulling will fetch the latest changes on GitHub and merge them with our local files. It’s best practice to pull often and to always pull BEFORE pushing. It makes sense to grab the latest version of the files on GitHub and then make our local edits on top of that. After we make our local edits, then we can push them back to GitHub. This will create a clean history of your repo.
This habit will really save you whenever you’re working in a repo with other collaborators, who may make edits when you’re away from your computer. By pulling frequently, you can avoid merge conflicts. You can always pull at any point in your workflow, so don’t hesitate to make it a habit.
With that said, click on the Downward Triangle at the top right corner.

Then click “Fetch origin” to fetch the latest changes from GitHub.

Nothing will happen; we’re already up to date because we haven’t done anything to our GitHub repo since we’ve last touched it.
If there were any changes on GitHub that our local repo doesn’t have yet, the “Pull origin” would automatically appear after fetching, but that is not the case right here.

Click either of the “Push origin” buttons to send our local changes to GitHub.

Now wait for GitHub Desktop to complete the process.

You’ve successfully pushed to GitHub! Click on “History” to confirm that we’ve made our second commit.

You should have a second commit where you created your practice script.

You can confirm by navigating back to your GitHub repo. Check to see that you now have a second commit where you created your practice script. If you click on where it says “2 Commits”, you can view the history of all your commits.

Click on your latest commit.

You should be able to see the green lines again showing the new additions to this file.




Congrats! You’ve now completed a Git workflow where you created a repo on GitHub, cloned it to your local computer, created a local file, and pushed it back to GitHub! You can rinse and repeat this workflow during the course of your projects. Remember, after you have your remote and local repos set up, the correct order is to make your local edits –> stage your local changes –> commit them with descriptive commit messages –> pull –> push to your remote repo.

title: MaloneLab Server
text:
The MaloneLab Server is a cloud storage location owned by YSE. All members of the lab should have access to this location to store large data files. The steps to access the server are as follows:

MacWindows



1️⃣ Contact the YSE IT team for access
Email the YSE IT team at ysehelpdesk@yale.edu to ask for access to corellia.environment.yale.edu/malonelab if Sparkle has not already asked for access.


2️⃣ Make sure you’re connected to Yale network or use the VPN
Connection to the server requires that your computer use the Yale on-campus networks (such as ethernet or one of the Yale Wifi networks). If you’re not on-campus, you may use the Yale VPN to route your traffic through Yale.
To use the Yale VPN, you will need the Cisco Secure Client VPN software. All Yale-managed computers should automatically have this software installed, but if you need Cisco Secure Client on a personal computer, visit this Yale IT Services walkthrough to see where to download the Cisco Secure Client.
Once you have Cisco Secure Client, simply open it up, and type “access.yale.edu” as the address. Click “Connect”

You might need to log in with your Yale email, NetID password, and do your two-factor authentication. After logging in, you will see this message:



3️⃣ Connect to server
Once you’re connected to the VPN, click “Go” at the top of your Mac screen. Then click “Connect to Server…”.

Type in “smb://corellia.environment.yale.edu/malonelab” in the address bar and click “Connect”.

And you should now be connected to the server! Research projects typically live under the “Research” folder.

This icon will appear on your desktop as long as you’re connected to the server.



4️⃣ Use the right file paths
Absolute file paths in the server will begin like:
/Volumes/MaloneLab/Research/...
If you have a my_project folder under Research, you can point to it in R like so:

# Option 1
server_folder <- "/Volumes/MaloneLab/Research/my_project"

# Option 2
server_folder <- file.path("/", "Volumes", "MaloneLab", "Research", "my_project")

Or in Python:
# Option 1
server_folder = "/Volumes/MaloneLab/Research/my_project"

# Option 2
from pathlib import Path
server_folder = Path("/", "Volumes", "MaloneLab", "Research", "my_project")




1️⃣ Contact the YSE IT team for access
Email the YSE IT team at ysehelpdesk@yale.edu to ask for access to corellia.environment.yale.edu/malonelab if Sparkle has not already asked for access.


2️⃣ Make sure you’re connected to Yale network or use the VPN
Connection to the server requires that your computer use the Yale on-campus networks (such as ethernet or one of the Yale Wifi networks). If you’re not on-campus, you may use the Yale VPN to route your traffic through Yale.
To use the Yale VPN, you will need the Cisco Secure Client VPN software. All Yale-managed computers should automatically have this software installed, but if you need Cisco Secure Client on a personal computer, visit this Yale IT Services walkthrough to see where to download the Cisco Secure Client.
Once you have Cisco Secure Client, simply open it up, and type “access.yale.edu” as the address. Click “Connect”

You might need to log in with your Yale email, NetID password, and do your two-factor authentication. After logging in, you will see this message:



3️⃣ Connect to server
Once you’re connected to the VPN, search for “Run” at Windows search bar and click to open the program.

Type in “\\corellia.environment.yale.edu\malonelab” in the address bar and click “Connect”.

And you should now be connected to the server! Research projects typically live under the “Research” folder.



4️⃣ Use the right file paths
Absolute file paths in the server will begin like:
\\corellia.environment.yale.edu\MaloneLab\Research\...
If you have a my_project folder under Research, you can point to it in R like so:

# Option 1
server_folder <- "//corellia.environment.yale.edu/MaloneLab/Research/my_project"

# Option 2
server_folder <- file.path("/", "corellia.environment.yale.edu", "MaloneLab", "Research", "my_project")

Or in Python:
# Option 1
server_folder = "//corellia.environment.yale.edu/MaloneLab/Research/my_project"

# Option 2
from pathlib import Path
server_folder = Path("/", "corellia.environment.yale.edu", "MaloneLab", "Research", "my_project")










Note



It’s best to avoid typing file paths with “/” or “\” because this can differ between operating systems, but this is unavoidable with MaloneLab Server paths (unless you set your working directory to a folder inside the server). If you must use slashes in your code, use the forward slash “/”, as this will work on both Mac and Windows.
For more tips on file paths, please check out File Paths.

title: Other Tips
text: There is no shortage of tips you can follow for a more organized, clean, and reproducible workflow. This page consolidates some other tips that may be helpful to your project!

title: Other Tips
text:
Naming Conventions

You can choose any naming convention you want— the best thing is to just be consistent about it!
Make sure your folder/file names are informative, but not overly long

The names should give just enough information about the folder/file contents and purpose

Avoid special characters and spaces to increase machine readability

Can replace spaces with _ or -
Windows computers in particular can run into trouble if there’s any whitespace in file names while reading file paths

You can add step numbers to your workflow scripts if they’re meant to be run in a certain order like: 01_data_download.R, 02_data_prep.R, 03_data_analysis.R, etc.
You can also append letters to the step numbers to signal the scripts belong to the same stage of the workflow

For example, 01a_precip_data_download.R, 01b_airtemp_data_download.R, etc.

title: Other Tips
text:
Folder Organization

This may be obvious, but it’s ideal to have sub-folders to clearly represent different parts of your project

For instance, you may have folders such as workflows, functions, miscellaneous, data, and deprecated across your repos

See https://github.com/lter/lterwg-flux-gradient for inspiration
Be careful not to put the wrong files in each folder; you should keep only relevant files in the right folder throughout your project lifecycle

If there’s no right folder to put your new files in, create a new folder! Avoid putting stray files at the top-most level of your project directory whenever you can’t find a good place for them.

You can explain the structure of your folders in the repo’s README so that anyone coming across your repo will know where to find what files.

title: Welcome!
text:
Welcome to the Malone Disturbance Ecology Lab (MDEL)! Here you’ll find various resources designed to help you in setting up your projects and integrating with the lab. There is a lot of content, but you will probably want to check out this landing page, Setup, and Best Practices at the very least.
Please don’t hesitate to reach out if you have any questions or feedback on this website.

title: Welcome!
text:
Lab Policies 📄
Please take a moment to read, review, and sign the lab policies here. Thank you!

title: Welcome!
text:
Directory 🗂️
Main lab website: https://www.malonelab.org/
MDEL GitHub organization: https://github.com/Malone-Disturbance-Ecology-Lab
MDEL DataONE portal: https://search.dataone.org/portals/3107251/About
For help in setting up your new project properly, visit the tabs under Setup.
For ideas on how to best format your workflow for maximum reproducibility, visit the tabs under Best Practices.
Lastly, check out the tabs under Resources for a list of technical resources, such as tutorials on coding, data publication, synthesis science, website creation, HPC clusters, and more!

title: File Paths
text:
In general, file paths should be saved to its own variables near the top of the script, or marked clearly somewhere so that users can easily change paths if needed.
If you’re consistently reusing the same base folders, you can save them in their own respective variables near the top of the script and just add on the inner folders/files as needed with the file.path() function in R or the pathlib module in Python. This will be helpful if you’re constantly switching between your local files and the files on the MaloneLab Server (more on that below).

title: File Paths
text:
Local Paths

RPython


Imagine your GitHub repo is named my_project. If you’ve followed the setup instructions, you should have cloned the repo to your local computer via Rprojects. In RStudio, once you open the .Rproj file associated with your local Git repo, your current working directory will automatically be set to the project directory. Thus, you can immediately start using relative file paths instead of specifying your paths beginning from the root directory.
For example, let’s say that we have a folder in my_project that holds our downloaded data called downloaded_data. Then inside, there’s a landsat_data folder that has—you guessed it—our Landsat data. For now, we have Landsat bands 1 and 2 downloaded as .tif files under the B01 and B02 folders, and our folder structure looks like this:

Since our current working directory is automatically set to the my_project folder, we can use relative file paths and read in our local files like so:

library(terra)

# Point to Landsat folder
landsat_path <- file.path("downloaded_data", "landsat_data")

# Read in Band 1 raster
band1_raster <- terra::rast(file.path(landsat_path, "B01", "example_raster.tif"))

# Read in Band 2 raster
band2_raster <- terra::rast(file.path(landsat_path, "B02", "another_example_raster.tif"))



Imagine your GitHub repo is named my_project. If you’ve followed the setup instructions, you should have cloned the repo to your local computer via the VS Code GUI or GitHub Desktop. In VS Code or your preferred Python IDE, once you open the folder associated with your local Git repo, your current working directory will automatically be set to the project directory. Thus, you can immediately start using relative file paths instead of specifying your paths beginning from the root directory.
For example, let’s say that we have a folder in my_project that holds our downloaded data called downloaded_data. Then inside, there’s a landsat_data folder that has—you guessed it—our Landsat data. For now, we have Landsat bands 1 and 2 downloaded as .tif files under the B01 and B02 folders, and our folder structure looks like this:

Since our current working directory is automatically set to the my_project folder, we can use relative file paths and read in our local files like so:
import rasterio
from pathlib import Path

# Point to Landsat folder
landsat_path = Path("downloaded_data", "landsat_data")

# Read in Band 1 raster
band1_raster = rasterio.open(landsat_path.joinpath("B01", "example_raster.tif"))

# Read in Band 2 raster
band2_raster = rasterio.open(landsat_path.joinpath("B02", "another_example_raster.tif"))

# Close them when you're done
band1_raster.close()
band2_raster.close()



Notice how we did not need to use any forward or backward slashes at all. If someone cloned your repo and put the data files in the right folders, they would not need to change the file paths in your script at all, regardless of what operating system they use.

title: File Paths
text:
Server Paths






Warning



If you’re working on a Windows computer, be sure to use corellia.environment.yale.edu instead of Volumes in your file paths!


Now what if your research is at the stage where you would like other lab members to run your workflow, but maybe you don’t necessarily want them to re-download the entire dataset since it takes a long time? You can create a folder on the MaloneLab Server for your project with the same inner folder structure. Below is an example of how your folders can look like in the server. Then either move or copy your data files here.

Now everyone in the lab has access to these files.

RPython


Your working directory is most likely still set to your local computer.
So one way to point to server paths in R is to use the absolute file paths, which can look like this:

# On Mac:
landsat_path <- "/Volumes/MaloneLab/Research/my_project/downloaded_data/landsat_data"

# Or on Windows:
landsat_path <- "//corellia.environment.yale.edu/MaloneLab/Research/my_project/downloaded_data/landsat_data"

# The forward slash "/" in file paths for R & Python scripts will work on both Mac and Windows

but alternatively, we can also use file.path() as good practice and to easily add on our inner folders/files.
In that case, we can adjust our scripts to read in server files like so:

library(terra)

# Point to Landsat folder
# If you're using a Windows computer, use "corellia.environment.yale.edu" instead of "Volumes"
landsat_path <- file.path("/", "Volumes", "MaloneLab", "Research", "my_project", "downloaded_data", "landsat_data")

# Read in Band 1 raster
band1_raster <- terra::rast(file.path(landsat_path, "B01", "example_raster.tif"))

# Read in Band 2 raster
band2_raster <- terra::rast(file.path(landsat_path, "B02", "another_example_raster.tif"))

If you want to easily switch between your local computer and the MaloneLab Server, you can even leave in both options and allow yourself or whoever is running your script to decide by commenting out whichever path they’re not using.

# Leave in both the local and server paths and let the user decide

# Comment this out if using the server files
landsat_path <- file.path("downloaded_data", "landsat_data")

# Comment this out if using the local files
# If you're using a Windows computer, use "corellia.environment.yale.edu" instead of "Volumes"
landsat_path <- file.path("/", "Volumes", "MaloneLab", "Research", "my_project", "downloaded_data", "landsat_data")



Your working directory is most likely still set to your local computer.
So one way to point to server paths in R is to use the absolute file paths, which can look like this:
# On Mac:
landsat_path = "/Volumes/MaloneLab/Research/my_project/downloaded_data/landsat_data"

# Or on Windows:
landsat_path = "//corellia.environment.yale.edu/MaloneLab/Research/my_project/downloaded_data/landsat_data"

# The forward slash "/" in file paths for R & Python scripts will work on both Mac and Windows
but alternatively, we can also use the pathlib module as good practice and to easily add on our inner folders/files.
In that case, we can adjust our scripts to read in server files like so:
import rasterio
from pathlib import Path

# Point to Landsat folder
# If you're using a Windows computer, use "corellia.environment.yale.edu" instead of "Volumes"
landsat_path = Path("/", "Volumes", "MaloneLab", "Research", "my_project", "downloaded_data", "landsat_data")

# Read in Band 1 raster
band1_raster = rasterio.open(landsat_path.joinpath("B01", "example_raster.tif"))

# Read in Band 2 raster
band2_raster = rasterio.open(landsat_path.joinpath("B02", "another_example_raster.tif"))

# Close them when you're done
band1_raster.close()
band2_raster.close()
If you want to easily switch between your local computer and the MaloneLab Server, you can even leave in both options and allow yourself or whoever is running your script to decide by commenting out whichever path they’re not using.
# Leave in both the local and server paths and let the user decide

# Comment this out if using the server files
landsat_path = Path("downloaded_data", "landsat_data")

# Comment this out if using the local files
# If you're using a Windows computer, use "corellia.environment.yale.edu" instead of "Volumes"
landsat_path = Path("/", "Volumes", "MaloneLab", "Research", "my_project", "downloaded_data", "landsat_data")



Since the inner folder structure is the same between your local computer and the server, there’s no need to go into your scripts and manually change the paths that build off of the base folders.
There may be other scenarios where you may use a mix of local and server file paths in the same script. It’s worth the time to plan ahead and organize your folders accordingly!






Note



These tips are not rules you must absolutely follow, but rather guidelines meant to help increase the reproducibility of your scripts by lessening the burden for whoever wants to rerun your work in the future (yes, that can even include future-you rerunning your own research on a different machine!)

title: Installation
text: Before you can begin on your new project, please complete the preparation steps relevant to your situation below. This will ensure that you will have all the tools/software needed for success!

title: Installation
text:
1️⃣ Create a GitHub Account
Create an account on GitHub if you don’t have one already. GitHub is a cloud-based platform and website where people can safely store and collaborate on code. You may have heard of it in relation to the software development world, but it is also huge in scientific research! Its useful capabilities will no doubt help elevate your workflow.
Once you have your account, log in and make sure you’re added to the MDEL GitHub Organization. All the relevant repositories belonging to the lab lives here. Once you’re added, you will have permission to create and edit these repositories.

title: Installation
section: 2️⃣ Install R / Python / Coding language of your choice
text:
2️⃣ Install R / Python / Coding language of your choice
Install R on your computer if you plan on using it. If you already have R and your R version is outdated by a few years, it is worth updating it at this point. Please ensure that your R version is at least 4.0.0 before proceeding to the next step.
Install Python on your computer if you plan on using it. Again, if you already have Python, you might want to check to make sure you’re not using a super outdated version.
Feel free to install any other coding languages as well.

title: Installation
section: 3️⃣ Install RStudio / Visual Studio Code / IDE of your choice
text:
3️⃣ Install RStudio / Visual Studio Code / IDE of your choice
Install RStudio if you plan on using R. RStudio is currently the most popular integrated development environment (IDE) for R users, but it can also run Python.
You may also download other IDEs if you wish.
Another popular IDE is Visual Studio Code. This IDE is popular across lots of coding languages in general, and is a great choice if you plan on using various languages.
This guide will have explicit tutorials geared towards RStudio and Visual Studio Code users, but even if you don’t wish to use these, you may still find useful tips that can be applied to your own IDE of choice (see the “GitHub Desktop” section in step 7).

title: Installation
section: 4️⃣ Check if you already have Git
text:
4️⃣ Check if you already have Git
If you’re using a Mac computer, there’s a good chance that it already has Git.
Run this command line code to check if your computer already has Git. You can run this in the Terminal on Mac or Linux. If you’re using a Windows computer, and you don’t know if you have an equivalent shell, you can open up RStudio or VS Code and run this command in the “Terminal” tab/window.
which git
If this command returns a file path, then you already have Git and can skip the Git installation. Otherwise, proceed to install Git.

title: Installation
section: 5️⃣ Install Git if you don’t have it already
text:
5️⃣ Install Git if you don’t have it already
Git is an open-source version control software designed to track changes in files and allow other people to collaborate on your work. The way to install Git will be different depending on your operating system, so look through and pick the right option for your machine.
Be sure to close any IDEs you have open before installing Git, as this can cause your IDE to not be able to detect Git later on.

MacWindowsLinux


Run the following command line code.
xcode-select --install
For more detailed instructions, see here.


Install Git for Windows (a.k.a. “Git Bash”). When asked about “Adjusting your PATH environment”, select “Git from the command line and also from 3rd-party software”.
For more detailed instructions, see here.


Install Git via your distro’s package manager (in the Terminal).
If you use Ubuntu or Debian Linux that code is as follows:
sudo apt-get install git
If instead you use Fedora or RedHat Linux the code is instead:
sudo yum install git
For more detailed instructions, see here.

title: Installation
text:
6️⃣ Check that Git is installed
Run this command line code to check that your computer has Git. You can run this in the Terminal on Mac or Linux, or the Git Bash shell if you’ve installed Git for Windows from the previous step.
which git

title: Installation
text:
7️⃣ Connect your IDE and Git/GitHub
In order to send code from your computer to GitHub, you will need to authenticate yourself so that GitHub knows you’re actually the one making these changes. There are several ways to do this: the Personal Access Token (PAT), Secure Shell (SSH), or a browser-based OAuth authentication. We will focus on the PAT and OAuth methods for this walkthrough. For a more thorough breakdown of SSH, please see Jenny Bryan’s “HTTPS versus SSH” page.
Follow one of the RStudio or VS Code or GitHub Desktop tutorials for this step. You do not need to follow all 3.
If you don’t use RStudio or VS Code, try out GitHub Desktop instead!

RStudioVS CodeGitHub Desktop


For this section, you can run the commands in the “Console” tab in RStudio.
First, make sure you have the usethis and gitcreds R packages installed.

# Install the `usethis` and `gitcreds` packages
install.packages(c("usethis", "gitcreds"))

Then, update your Git profile on your computer. Replace "Your GitHub Username" with your actual GitHub username and replace "Your GitHub Email" with your actual email you use for GitHub. Once you filled those out, run the command.

# Add your GitHub username and email
usethis::use_git_config(user.name = "Your GitHub Username",
                        user.email = "Your GitHub Email")

Finally, create your Personal Access Token (PAT). You can run the command below to direct you to GitHub’s page to create a new token. Once you’re on that page, make sure to check the checkboxes to give yourself the necessary permissions. (You can just check everything if you’re not sure).
When you get to the expiration date step, you may set it to 90 days, after which you will need to regenerate your token again. You will automatically get an email notification from GitHub when your token is approaching its expiration date.
For more help on PAT, check out steps 6-10 in GitHub’s PAT tutorial.

# Create a token (Note this will open a GitHub browser tab)
## See steps 6-10 in GitHub's PAT tutorial 
usethis::create_github_token()







Note



This command will prompt you to create a “Classic” token on GitHub, but there are also a second type of token called “Fine-Grained” tokens. Fine-grained tokens will allow you to customize some very specific permissions, but Classic tokens will work just as well.


After generating your token, copy it. You may also want to paste it into a secure document to safely save it for future use. Head back to RStudio, type out and run this command. It will prompt you for your token. Paste it into the Console.

# Now, give your token to RStudio
## After you run this line follow the prompts in the "Console" tab of RStudio
gitcreds::gitcreds_set()

Now your computer will remember your token for the next 30, 60, 90, or so odd-days! Once your token expires, you will need to regenerate it and repeat this step.
There you go! You should now be ready to do your workflow. Head to Git Workflow for the next step.


There are several ways you can connect your VS Code session and Git/GitHub through VS Code’s handy interface! This guide will simply go over one possible method. For more options, feel free to read VS Code’s documentation.
First, update your Git profile on your computer. You can do this by selecting Terminal –> New Terminal. Then, in the Terminal, run this command, but replace “Your GitHub Username” with your actual GitHub username.
git config --global user.name "Your GitHub Username"
Next, run this command, but replace “Your GitHub Email” with your actual email you use for GitHub.
git config --global user.email "Your GitHub Email"
You can check your Git profile settings by running git config -list in the Terminal just to double-check.
Now click on your account menu in the bottom left corner of VS Code. Go to Backup and Sync Settings… –> Sign in –> Sign in with GitHub.
A new window should have popped up to allow you to authorize VS Code. Follow the on-screen prompts to finish the browser-based OAuth authorization process.
Due to the way VS Code works, we will still need to finish the last of the authorization steps later on, so hang tight and proceed to Git Workflow for the next step.


GitHub Desktop allows you to do the typical Git workflow, no matter what IDE you use. Since it is IDE-agnostic, it is extremely flexible in a variety of situations, even if your IDE does not have a dedicated GitHub feature.
Go download GitHub Desktop and follow Part 1 of their user guide to get started.
Then head to Git Workflow for the next step.

title: README Help
text:
The README is a great place to write a summary of your project objectives, folders, workflows, and any other information that would be important for others to know about your project. GitHub repos will automatically display the README at the landing page of your repo so it will be the first thing that everyone sees about your project. It’s well worth the time to update the README regularly,
If you didn’t check the option to create a README during repo initialization, you can always create one at any time by going to the repo landing page on GitHub then Add File –> Create New File –> name the file “README.md”. Or you can also name it “README.Rmd” if you want your README to run R code.
READMEs will look different depending on the type of project. Check out the different README examples below for a…

personal research project: https://github.com/jnawang/thermal_acclimation_in_ecosystem_respiration
synthesis working group: https://github.com/lter/lterwg-flux-gradient
R package: https://github.com/Malone-Disturbance-Ecology-Lab/ERA5Flux

In general, consider adding these to your README if applicable:

Repo purpose

Write a summary of the purpose of your repo. What is the project you’re doing? What is the rationale behind it?

Repo structure or folder organization

If you have sub-folders in your repo (and you should!), you should explain what each folder is for so that users visiting your repo will understand how your work is organized. You may even “draw” a directory structure like so:


my_project/
├── functions/               # Core analysis functions
│   ├── summarize_data.R             
│   └── plot_results.R             
│
├── workflows/               # Complete analysis workflows
│   ├── data_download.R   
│   ├── data_prep.R        
│   └── data_analysis.R             
│
├── exploratory/             # Preliminary analyses and development
│   └── ...
│
└── deprecated/              # Unused workflows and functions

Script explanations

Write short descriptions of what your scripts do. If you anticipate that your workflow will involve many inputs and outputs, you can list the inputs and outputs of each script to keep track of the moving parts.

Details on accessing external data required for workflow (if any)

Make sure to include any necessary links if needed.

Instructions on downloading special software required for workflow (if any)

For example, if your workflow requires software hosted only on GitHub, you can include the code to download that software.

References or acknowledgments

Give credit where credit is due.

title: Where to Put Data
text:
Planning out where to store your data is a task worth doing, especially since your data could end up in multiple locations throughout the duration of your project. Below are some guidelines designed to help you start thinking about where to store your data for maximum reproducibility.

Large datasets (hundreds of GBs) belong to MaloneLab Server
Small or medium datasets (MBs or tens of GBs) can be kept locally or on the server
Generally best to not store data files on GitHub!! GitHub has a hard file size limit and will prevent you from uploading anything over 100MB

If you must store on GitHub, make sure the file is small

Where to put data depends not only on file size, but also your expectation for the person looking to reproduce your results

If your research hasn’t been published yet, it may make sense to point file paths to server so Sparkle and the other lab members can run your workflow. There’s a high possibility that your workflow will include a data download step that may take a long time, so it would be helpful if the data is already in the server for the lab members to use in subsequent workflow steps (if they don’t want to wait for data to download).
If your research is in the final stages of publication, it may make sense to point file paths to the local directory so when the general public clones your repo, they can run scripts to download/import/export data on their local computer.
If you think you will switch paths a lot between your local computer and the server, it may suit you to have identical folder structure for your project on your local computer and on the server. Then in your scripts you can specify a base folder (either a local folder or a server folder), and keep the rest of the inner file paths the same (more on that in File Paths).
